Q1: I organized my study routine around hands-on practice more than anything else. I'd dedicate huge chunks of time—sometimes 6 or 7 hours in a day—to working through problems and actually doing things rather than just reading. My attendance was terrible, I know, but I made up for it by putting in around 38 hours a week of independent study. I'd often work late at night when my anxiety was lower and I could focus better without distractions.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally. Some weeks I'd be incredibly consistent, studying every evening after dinner. Other weeks, when the stress and sadness hit hard, I'd have to cram everything closer to deadlines. I tried to maintain regularity, but life and my mental state didn't always cooperate with that plan.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or seeing if grades had been posted. I logged in pretty frequently—maybe three times a day on busy weeks—because I was anxious about missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was staying on top of things, even if I didn't actually do much once I was there. The fear of falling behind kept me checking constantly.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became almost automatic after the first few weeks. I'd wake up, check my phone, then check the LMS before even getting out of bed. It became part of my daily ritual, especially because I wasn't attending classes in person much. The LMS was basically my main connection to the course, so checking it felt necessary for survival.
Q5: The practice exercises and any interactive content were the most useful for me because I learn by doing. Videos were helpful too—I watched a decent amount—but only when I could pause and try things myself alongside them. Discussion posts were surprisingly useful because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. I needed to physically engage with material, not just passively absorb it.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials and some of the supplementary articles. They felt too passive for how I learn best, and honestly, when you're already stressed and sad, staring at walls of text just makes everything worse. I'd skim them at most or look for summaries elsewhere. If something didn't feel immediately applicable to assignments or exams, it got pushed to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to something I'd be assessed on or if it helped me actually understand a concept I was struggling with. Practical application was key—if I could do something with my hands or work through a problem step by step, I was engaged. Activities that felt like busywork or pure theory without application just drained my already limited mental energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When you're putting in 38 hours a week and dealing with high anxiety, you have to triage ruthlessly. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones only happened if I had spare energy and they seemed genuinely interesting. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff, which just added to my stress.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything and worked offline constantly. I'd print out problem sets and work through them on paper, or I'd have PDFs open on my tablet while I practiced with physical materials. A lot of my real learning happened away from the computer entirely. I also used YouTube tutorials and external websites to supplement what the course provided.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened when I was working through problems on paper or practicing concepts physically. I'd download a lecture, watch it once, then spend hours afterward working through related problems without touching the LMS. The system would show I accessed something briefly, but it couldn't see the four hours I spent afterward wrestling with the material at my desk.
Q11: All the time. I'd download videos and documents, watch or read them once online, and then work with those materials offline for days. My internet is decent but I preferred having everything locally so I could study without distractions. The LMS would show one access, but that material might have been central to a whole week of my studying.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my sadness and anxiety made everything harder. Mid-semester was particularly rough—I felt overwhelmed by the workload across all my courses, and some personal stuff was weighing on me. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. I'd force myself to do the minimum required and just try to survive until things felt more manageable.
Q13: My anxiety definitely affected how I used the LMS. Sometimes I'd log in multiple times checking the same things repeatedly because I was worried I'd missed something. Other times, when the stress peaked, I'd avoid logging in entirely because seeing my to-do list made me feel worse. Fatigue from long study sessions meant I'd sometimes access materials but not really process them properly.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. There were times I'd have videos playing or documents open, but my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about other things or just too exhausted to focus. I'd technically be 'studying' but nothing was actually sinking in. Those sessions felt like going through the motions, and I'd have to redo them later when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot, even though I knew peer interaction was valuable. Putting my thoughts out there for others to see triggered my anxiety. I'd also avoid starting big assignments until closer to deadlines because the scope of them felt paralyzing. It's not that I didn't care—I cared too much, and that caring sometimes manifested as avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. They created structure that I desperately needed, but they also spiked my anxiety significantly. I'd often have bursts of intense LMS activity in the days before something was due. Without deadlines, I might have spread my work out more evenly, but I also might have let things slip entirely. They kept me accountable even when I resented them.
Q17: Feedback was crucial for me. When I got positive feedback or saw improvement in my scores, it motivated me to engage more deeply. But when feedback was delayed or minimal, I felt lost and my anxiety increased. I'd sometimes check the LMS obsessively waiting for grades to be posted. Good feedback gave me hope; its absence made me spiral into worry about whether I was on the right track.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my 38 hours of weekly study. But the structure also felt rigid sometimes—I couldn't always engage when the course wanted me to because my mental state didn't align with the schedule. I adapted by front-loading work when I felt good and coasting when I didn't.
Q19: The availability of downloadable resources made engagement easier because I could study in my own way, offline. Having practice exercises was great for my kinesthetic learning style. What made it harder was when content was only accessible through streaming or required constant online presence. Also, the sheer volume of material across my 17 courses sometimes made it hard to give each one proper attention.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is struggling to understand the material or feeling anxious about it. They might keep accessing resources, rewatching videos, rereading notes, but never feel confident enough to actually submit work. It could also be someone dealing with perfectionism or fear of failure—they're engaged with learning but paralyzed when it comes to being evaluated.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was only about 5%, which would look terrible on paper, but I still earned a Merit grade. I did most of my learning offline, through independent study and practice. The LMS activity doesn't capture someone downloading everything and then spending hours working with those materials away from the system. Presence doesn't equal learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you log in, what you access. They're good at tracking surface-level engagement. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, the emotional state behind it, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing while they stared at the wall feeling sad.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about that feeling when a concept finally clicks after you've worked through it hands-on. It's showing up mentally even when you don't feel like it, pushing through the anxiety and sadness to keep learning. True engagement is internal—it's caring about understanding, not just completing.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally while maintaining my performance. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline practice, the anxiety attacks before submitting assignments, or the resilience it took to keep going when I felt sad and overwhelmed. It wouldn't show that my high engagement came at a significant personal cost, or that sometimes my 'low activity' periods were actually me recovering so I could engage properly later.
